insights.insight: The ad industry pays $5–$150 CPA for probabilistic attribution that infers purchase intent. A cryptographically verified receipt eliminates the inference entirely — the conversion event is the signal, not a model of it.

Chrome's deprecation of third-party cookies removes the last cross-site tracking primitive shared by Safari and Firefox. No current replacement — fingerprinting, first-party data, or broker segments — produces identity-free, cross-ecosystem purchase verification.

Amazon, Visa, Google, and Meta each see a fragment of the purchase journey but none sees across all of them. Receipt-layer data sits below every platform silo and captures what each misses — confirmed purchase, product, and retailer in a single signal.

The dataset has shifted from SaaS-dominant toward physical retail, adding Home Depot and Starbucks receipts across two consecutive snapshots. Physical receipts are harder to fake, carry higher transaction values, and are directly relevant to CPG and retail advertiser use cases.

Consumer-submitted receipts enter the dataset without brand opt-in — a Starbucks receipt is valid whether Starbucks participates or not. Brands that stay out are still measured; participation is the only way to influence how that data is used.

Receipt intake fell 17% and GMV dropped 13% in a single snapshot window — the steepest contraction recorded — though acceleration has since recovered to 1.001x. Velocity at 0.597x remains compressed, signaling the dataset's growth trajectory is real but not yet stable.
